pages.title: The functors \(\Gamma_Z\), \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\)
pages.html:
1 The functors \(\Gamma_Z\), \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\)

Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_X\) the category of abelian sheaves on \(X\). Let \(\Phi\) be a family of supports in the sense of Cartan; one defines the functor \(\Gamma_\Phi\) on \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_X\) by:

\[ \tag{1} { \Gamma_\Phi(F) = \text{ subgroup of } \Gamma(F) \text{ consisting of the sections } f \text{ such that support } f \in \Phi}. \]

If \(Z\) is a closed subset of \(X\), we denote, by abuse of language, by \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z\) the functor \(\Gamma_\Phi\), where \(\Phi\) is the set of closed subsets of \(X\) contained in \(Z\). Hence we have:

\[ \tag{2} {\Gamma_Z(F) = \text{ subgroup of } \Gamma(F) \text{ consisting of the sections } f \text{ such that support } f \subset Z}. \]

We wish to generalize this definition to the case where \(Z\) is a locally closed subset of \(X\), hence closed in a suitable open subset \(V\) of \(X\). In this case one sets:

\[ \tag{3} {\Gamma_Z(F)=\Gamma_Z(F|_{V})}. \]

One must check that \(\Gamma_Z(F)\) "does not depend" on the chosen open set. It suffices to show that if \(V'\), \(V\supset V' \supset Z\) is an open set, then the map \(\rho_{V'}^V: F(V) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(V')\) maps \(\Gamma_Z(F|_{V})\) isomorphically onto \(\Gamma_Z(F|_{V'})\). Now

\[ \tag{4} { \Gamma_Z(F|_{V}) = \ker \rho_{V-Z}^V} \]

hence if \(f\in \Gamma_Z(F|_{V})\) and if \(\rho_{V'}^V(f)=\rho_{V-Z}^V(f)=0\) then \(f=0\) since \((V', V-Z)\) is a covering of \(V\). Likewise, if \(f'\in \Gamma_Z(F|_{V'})\), then \(f'\in F(V')\) and \(0\in F(V-Z)\) define an \(f\in F(V)\) such that \(\rho_{V'}^V(f)=f', f\in \Gamma_Z(F|_{V})\), hence \(\rho_{V'}^V\) induces an isomorphism \(\Gamma_Z(F|_{V}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_Z(F|_{V'})\).

Note that every open set \(W\) of \(Z\) is induced by an open set \(U\) of \(X\) in which \(W\) is closed. It follows that \(W \rightsquigarrow \Gamma_W(F)\) defines a presheaf on \(Z\), and one checks that it is a sheaf, which we shall denote by \(i^!(F)\), where \(i: Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the canonical immersion. One finds:

\[ \tag{5} \Gamma_Z(F)=\Gamma(i^!(F)). \]

The sheaf \(i^!(F)\) is a subsheaf of \(i^*(F)\); indeed the canonical homomorphism:

\[ \Gamma(F|_{U})=\Gamma(U, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(U\cap Z, i^*(F)) \]

is injective on \(\Gamma_{U\cap Z}(F|_{U}) \subset \Gamma(F|_{U})\). Summing up, we have the following result:

Proposition 1.1

There exists a unique subsheaf \(i^!(F)\) of \(i^*(F)\) such that for every open set \(U\) of \(X\) such that \(U\cap Z\) is closed in \(U\),

\[ \Gamma(F|_{U})=\Gamma(U, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(U\cap Z, i^*(F)) \]

induces an isomorphism \(\Gamma_{U \cap Z}(F|_{U}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(U\cap Z, i^!(F))\).

Note that if \(Z\) is an open set one will simply have

\[ \tag{6} {i^!(F)=i^*(F)=F|_{Z}, \ \Gamma_Z(F)=\Gamma(Z, F)}. \]

Suppose again that \(Z\) is arbitrary. Then for a variable open set \(U\) of \(X\), one sees that

\[ U \rightsquigarrow \Gamma_{U\cap Z}(F|_{U})=\Gamma(U\cap Z, i^!(F)) \]

is a sheaf on \(X\), which we shall denote by \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)\); more precisely, according to the preceding formula (expressing that \(i^!\) commutes with restriction to open sets) one has an isomorphism

\[ \tag{7} {\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)= i_*(i^!(F))} \]

by definition, one has, for every open set \(U\) of \(X\),

\[ \tag{8} {\Gamma(U, \underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)) = \Gamma_{U\cap Z}(F|_{U})}. \]

Let us note here a characteristic difference between the case where \(Z\) is closed, and that where \(Z\) is open. In the first case, formula (8) shows us that \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)\) can be considered as a subsheaf of \(F\), and one thus has a canonical immersion

\[ \tag{8'} {\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F) \hookrightarrow F}. \]

In the case where \(Z\) is open, on the contrary, one sees from (6) that the second member of (8) is \(\Gamma(U\cap Z, F)\), hence receives \(\Gamma(U, F)\), so that one has a canonical homomorphism in the opposite direction to the preceding one:

\[ \tag{8} {F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)}, \]

which is moreover none other than the canonical homomorphism

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*(F), \]

taking into account the isomorphism

\[ \tag{6 bis} {\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)\simeq i_*i^*(F)} \]

deduced from (6) and (7).

Of course, for variable \(F\), \(\Gamma_Z(F)\), \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)\), \(i^!(F)\) can be considered as functors in \(F\), with values respectively in the category of abelian groups, of abelian sheaves on \(X\), of abelian sheaves on \(Z\). It is sometimes convenient to interpret the functor

\[ i^!: \underline{\mathrm{C}}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_Z \]

as the adjoint functor of a well-known functor

\[ i_!: \underline{\mathrm{C}}_Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{C}}_X \]

defined by the following proposition:

Proposition 1.2

Let \(G\) be an abelian sheaf on \(Z\). Then there exists a unique subsheaf of \(i_*(G)\), say \(i_!(G)\), such that for every open set \(U\) of \(X\) the (identical) isomorphism

\[ \Gamma(U\cap Z, G)= \Gamma(U, i_*(G)) \]

defines an isomorphism

\[ \Gamma_{\Phi_{U\cap Z, U}}(U\cap Z, G)=\Gamma(U, i_! (G)), \]

where \(\Phi_{U\cap Z, U}\) denotes the set of subsets of \(U\cap Z\) which are closed in \(U\).

The verification reduces to noting that the first member is a sheaf for variable \(U\), i.e. that the property, for a section of \(i_*(G)\) over \(U\), considered as a section of \(G\) over \(U\cap Z\), of having closed support in \(U\) is of local nature on \(U\). The sheaf \(i_!(G)\) just defined is also known under the name: sheaf deduced from \(G\) by extension by \(0\) outside \(Z\), cf. Godement. In particular, if \(Z\) is closed, one has

\[ \tag{9} {i_!(G)=i_*(G)}; \]

but in the general case, the canonical injection \(i_!(G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*(G)\) is not an isomorphism, as is already well known for \(Z\) open. Evidently, \(i_!(G)\) depends functorially on \(G\) (and it is even an exact functor in G). This said, one has:

Proposition 1.3

There exists an isomorphism of bifunctors in \(G, F\) (\(G\) abelian sheaf on \(Z\), \(F\) abelian sheaf on \(X\)):

\[ \tag{10} {\operatorname{Hom}(i_!(G), F) = \operatorname{Hom} (G, i^!(F))}. \]

To define such an isomorphism, it amounts to the same to define functorial homomorphisms

\[ i_!i^!(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F, \ G \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i^!i_!(G), \]

satisfying the well-known compatibility conditions (cf. for example Shih's talk at the Cartan seminar on cohomology operations).

Recalling that \(i_!\) is exact, hence transforms monomorphisms into monomorphisms, one concludes:

Corollary 1.4

If \(F\) is injective, \(i^!(F)\) is injective, hence \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^!(F)\) is also injective.

Replacing \(X\) by a variable open set \(U\) of \(X\), one also concludes from 1.3

Corollary 1.5

One has an isomorphism functorial in \(F, G\):

\[ \tag{11} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits{(i_!(G), F)} =i_*(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits{(G, i^!(F))}. \]

Taking for \(G\) the constant sheaf on \(Z\) defined by \(\mathbf{Z}\), say \(\mathbf{Z}_Z\), 1.3 and 1.5 specialize to

Corollary 1.6

One has isomorphisms functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{12} \begin{array}[c]{l} {\Gamma_Z(F)=\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{Z}_{Z, X}, F)}, \\ {\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(\mathbf{Z}_{Z, X}, F)}, \end{array} \]

where \(\mathbf{Z}_{Z, X}= i_!(\mathbf{Z}_Z)\) is the abelian sheaf on \(X\) deduced from the constant sheaf on \(Z\) defined by \(\mathbf{Z}\), by extension by \(0\) outside \(Z\).

Remark 1.7

Suppose that \(X\) is a ringed space, and endow \(Z\) with the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_Z=i^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X)\), finally denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_X\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}_Z\) the category of Modules on \(X\) resp. \(Z\). Then the preceding considerations extend word for word, taking for \(F\) a Module on \(X\) and for \(G\) a Module on \(Z\), and interpreting accordingly the statements 1.3 to 1.6.

To conclude these generalities, let us examine what happens when one changes the locally closed subset \(Z\). Let \(Z'\subset Z\) be another locally closed subset, and let

\[ j: Z' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z, i': Z' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X, i'=ij \]

be the canonical inclusions. Then one has functorial isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{13} {(ij)^!=j^!i^!, \quad (ij)_!=i_!j_!}. \]

The first isomorphism (13) defines a functorial isomorphism

\[ \tag{14} {\Gamma_{Z'}(F)=\Gamma(Z', (ij)^!(F))\simeq \Gamma(Z', j^!(i^!(F)))=\Gamma_{Z'}(i^!(F))}. \]

Suppose now that \(Z'\) is closed in \(Z\), and

\[ Z''=Z-Z' \]

its complement in \(Z\), which is open in \(Z\) hence locally closed in \(X\). The canonical inclusion (8') applied to \(i^!(F)\) on \(Z\) endowed with \(Z'\) defines for us, thanks to (14), a functorial injective canonical homomorphism

\[ \tag{15} {\Gamma_{Z'}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_Z(F)}. \]

If in (14) one replaces \(Z'\) by \(Z''\) and uses (8), one finds a functorial canonical homomorphism:

\[ \tag{15'} {\Gamma_Z(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{Z''}(F)}. \]
Proposition 1.8

Under the preceding conditions, the sequence of functorial homomorphisms:

\[ \tag{16} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{Z'}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_Z(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{Z''}(F)} \]

is exact. If \(F\) is flasque, the sequence remains exact upon putting a zero on the right.

Proof▾

Replacing \(X\) by an open set \(V\) in which \(Z\) is closed, one is reduced to the case where \(Z\) is closed, hence \(Z'\) closed. Then \(Z''\) is closed in the open set \(X-Z'\), and one has a canonical inclusion

\[ \Gamma_{Z''}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(X-Z', F), \]

and the exactness of (16) means simply that the sections of \(F\) with support in \(Z'\) are those whose restriction to \(X-Z'\) is zero.

When \(F\) is flasque, every element of \(\Gamma_{Z''}(F)\), considered as a section of \(F\) over \(X-Z'\), can be extended to a section of \(F\) over \(X\), and this latter will evidently have its support in \(Z\), which proves that then the last homomorphism in (16) is surjective.

Q.E.D.
Corollary 1.9

One has a functorial exact sequence

\[ \tag{16 bis} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_{Z'}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_Z(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_{Z''}(F)}, \]

and if \(F\) is flasque, this sequence remains exact upon putting a \(0\) on the right.

One can interpret (1.8) in terms of results on the functors \(\operatorname{Hom}\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits\) via 1.6, in the following way. Let us first note that if \(G\) is an abelian sheaf on \(Z\), inducing the sheaves \(j^*(G)\) and \(k^*(G)\) on \(Z'\) resp. \(Z''\) (where \(j: Z' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) and \(k: Z'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) are the canonical injections), one has a canonical exact sequence of sheaves on \(X\):

\[ \tag{17} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^*(G)_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j^*(G)_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0} \]

where, to simplify the notation, the index \(X\) denotes the sheaf on \(X\) obtained by extension by \(0\) in the complement of the space of definition of the sheaf under consideration. The exact sequence (17) generalizes a well-known exact sequence when \(Z=X\) (cf. Godement), and is moreover deduced from it by writing the exact sequence in question on \(Z\), and applying the functor \(i_!\). Taking \(G=\mathbf{Z}_{Z}\), one concludes in particular:

Proposition 1.10

Under the preceding conditions, one has an exact sequence of abelian sheaves on \(X\):

\[ \tag{18} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z}_{Z{''}, X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z}_{Z, X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z}_{Z', X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0}. \]

This being so, the two exact sequences 1.8 and 1.9 are none other than the exact sequences deduced from (18) by application of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, F)\) resp. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(-, F)\).

This gives anew an obvious proof of the fact that the sequences (16) and (16 bis) remain exact upon putting a zero on the right, provided that \(F\) is injective.

Corollary 1.9

One has a functorial exact sequence

\[ \tag{16 bis} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_{Z'}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_Z(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\Gamma}_{Z''}(F)}, \]

and if \(F\) is flasque, this sequence remains exact upon putting a \(0\) on the right.

One can interpret (1.8) in terms of results on the functors \(\operatorname{Hom}\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits\) via 1.6, in the following way. Let us first note that if \(G\) is an abelian sheaf on \(Z\), inducing the sheaves \(j^*(G)\) and \(k^*(G)\) on \(Z'\) resp. \(Z''\) (where \(j: Z' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) and \(k: Z'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) are the canonical injections), one has a canonical exact sequence of sheaves on \(X\):

\[ \tag{17} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k^*(G)_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j^*(G)_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0} \]

where, to simplify the notation, the index \(X\) denotes the sheaf on \(X\) obtained by extension by \(0\) in the complement of the space of definition of the sheaf under consideration. The exact sequence (17) generalizes a well-known exact sequence when \(Z=X\) (cf. Godement), and is moreover deduced from it by writing the exact sequence in question on \(Z\), and applying the functor \(i_!\). Taking \(G=\mathbf{Z}_{Z}\), one concludes in particular:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.10

Under the preceding conditions, one has an exact sequence of abelian sheaves on \(X\):

\[ \tag{18} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z}_{Z{''}, X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z}_{Z, X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Z}_{Z', X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0}. \]

This being so, the two exact sequences 1.8 and 1.9 are none other than the exact sequences deduced from (18) by application of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, F)\) resp. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(-, F)\).
This gives anew an obvious proof of the fact that the sequences (16) and (16 bis) remain exact upon putting a zero on the right, provided that \(F\) is injective.

pages.title: The functors \(\operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F)\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^*(F)\)
pages.html:
2 The functors \(\operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F)\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^*(F)\)

Definition 2.1

We denote by \(\operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F)\) and \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^*(F)\) the derived functors in \(F\) of the functors \(\Gamma_Z(F)\), resp. \(\underline{\Gamma}_Z(F)\).

These are cohomological functors, with values in the category of abelian groups, resp. in the category of abelian sheaves on \(X\). When \(Z\) is closed, \(H_Z^*(X, F)\) is by definition nothing other than \(H^*_{\Phi}(X, F)\), where \(\Phi\) denotes the family of closed subsets of \(X\) contained in \(Z\). When \(Z\) is open, we shall see that \(H_Z^*(X, F)\) is nothing other than \(H^*(Z, F)=H^*(Z, F|_{Z})\), thanks to the following proposition.

Proposition 2.2 (Excision theorem)

Let \(V\) be an open subset of \(X\) containing \(Z\). Then we have an isomorphism of cohomological functors in \(F\):

\[ \tag{19} \operatorname{H}^*_Z(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^*_Z(V, F|_{V}). \]

Indeed, we have a functorial isomorphism \(\Gamma_Z^X \simeq \Gamma_Z^V j^!\), where \(j: V \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the inclusion and where \(j^!\) is therefore the restriction functor (cf. (14)). The latter is exact, and transforms injectives into injectives by 1.4, whence at once the isomorphism (19).

When \(Z\) is open, we may take \(V=Z\) and we find:

Corollary 2.3

Suppose \(Z\) open, then we have an isomorphism of cohomological functors:

\[ \tag{20} \operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F) = \operatorname{H}^*(Z, F). \]

From the isomorphisms 1.6 and the definitions (cf. Tohoku) we conclude:

Proposition 2.3

We have isomorphisms of cohomological functors:

\begin{align*} \tag{21} \operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F)&\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^*(X; \mathbf{Z}_{Z, X}, F),\\ \tag{21 bis} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^*(F)&\simeq \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^*(\mathbf{Z}_{Z, X}, F). \end{align*}

We may therefore apply the results of Tohoku on the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) of Modules. Let us first point out the following interpretation of the sheaves \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^*(F)\) in terms of the global groups \(\operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F)\):

Corollary 2.4

\(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^*(F)\) is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf associated to the presheaf

\[ U \rightsquigarrow \operatorname{H}^*_{Z\cap U}(U, F|_{U}). \]

In particular, using corollary 2.3, we find:

Corollary 2.5

Suppose \(Z\) open, then we have an isomorphism of cohomological functors:

\[ \tag{22} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^*(F)=\operatorname{R}^*i_*i^*(F)} \]

(where \(i: Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the inclusion).

The spectral sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) gives the important spectral sequence:

Theorem 2.6

We have a spectral sequence functorial in \(F\), abutting to \(\operatorname{H}_Z^*(X, F)\) and with initial term

\[ \tag{23} { \operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}(F)=\operatorname{H}^p(X, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(F))}. \]
Remarks 2.7

It follows at once from 2.4 that the sheaves \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(F)\) are zero in \(X-\overline{Z}\), and likewise zero in the interior of \(Z\) for \(q\neq 0\) (so that for such a \(q\), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(F)\) is even supported on the boundary \(\overset{\circ}{Z}\) of \(Z\)).

Consequently, the right-hand side of (23) can be interpreted as a cohomology group on \(\overline{Z}\). We shall use 2.6 in the case where \(Z\) is closed in \(X\), and where the right-hand side of (23) can be interpreted as a cohomology group computed on \(Z\):

\[ \tag{23 bis} {\operatorname{E}_2^{p, q}(F) = \operatorname{H}^p(Z, \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^q(F))}. \]

Let us also note that when \(Z\) is open, the spectral sequence 2.6 is nothing other than the Leray spectral sequence for the continuous map \(i: Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), taking into account the interpretation 2.5 in the computation of the initial term of the Leray spectral sequence.

Let us take up again the exact sequence (1.10); it gives rise to an exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) (cf. Tohoku):

Theorem 2.8

Let \(Z\) be a locally closed subset of \(X\), \(Z'\) a closed subset of \(Z\) and \(Z''=Z-Z'\). Then we have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{24} \begin{gathered} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Z'}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Z^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Z''}^0(X, F)\xrightarrow{\partial} \operatorname{H}^1_{Z'}(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1_Z(X, F) \dots \\ \phantom{0}\dots \operatorname{H}_{Z'}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Z^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Z''}^i(X, F)\xrightarrow{\partial} \operatorname{H}^{i+1}_{Z'}(X, F)\dots. \end{gathered} \]

Let us recall how this exact sequence can be obtained. Let \(C(F)\) be an injective resolution of \(F\); then the exact sequence (1.10) gives rise to the exact sequence

\[ \tag{25} {0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{Z'}(C(F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(C(F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{Z''}(C(F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0}, \]

(which is nothing other than the one defined in 1.8). From it we conclude an exact cohomology sequence, which is nothing other than (24).

The most important case for us is the one where \(Z\) is closed (and one can moreover always reduce to it by replacing \(X\) by an open set \(V\) in which \(Z\) is closed). Then \(Z'\) is closed, \(Z''\) is closed in the open set \(X-Z'\), and we can write

\[ \tag{26} {\operatorname{H}^i_{Z''}(X, F)=\operatorname{H}^i_{Z''}(X-Z', F|_{X-Z'})}, \]

which allows us to write the exact sequence (24) in terms of cohomologies with support in a given closed set. The most frequent case is the one where \(Z=X\). Setting then, for simplicity, \(Z'=A\), we find:

Corollary 2.9

Let \(A\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Then we have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{27} \begin{gathered} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0_A(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X-A, F) \xrightarrow{\partial} \operatorname{H}^1_A(X, F) \dots\\ \phantom{0}\dots \operatorname{H}^i_A(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(X-A, F) \xrightarrow{\partial} \operatorname{H}^{i+1}_A(X, F)\dots. \end{gathered} \]

This exact sequence shows that the cohomology group \(\operatorname{H}^i_A(X, F)\) plays the role of a relative cohomology group of \(X\mod X-A\), with coefficients in \(F\). It is in this capacity that it was introduced naturally in applications. By "sheafifying" (24) and (27), or by proceeding directly, we find:

Corollary 2.10

Under the conditions of 2.8, we have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{24 bis} \dots \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_{Z'}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_{Z}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_{Z''}^i(F) \xrightarrow{\partial} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_{Z'}^{i+1}(F) \dots \]
Corollary 2.11

Let \(A\) be a closed subset of \(X\), then we have an exact sequence functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{28} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_A^0(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(F|_{X-A}) \xrightarrow{\partial} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_A^1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

and canonical isomorphisms, for \(i\geqslant 2\):

\[ \tag{29} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_A^i(F)= \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_{X-A}^{i-1}(F)=\operatorname{R}^{i-1}f_*(F|_{X-A}), \]

where \(f: (X-A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the inclusion.

This therefore defines \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^0_A(F)\) and \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_A^1(F)\) respectively as the \(\ker\) and \(\operatorname{coker}\) of the canonical homomorphism

\[ F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*f^*(F)= f_*(F|_{X-A}), \]

and the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_A^i(F)\) (\(i\geqslant 2\)) in terms of the derived functors of \(f_*\).

Corollary 2.12

Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(X\). If \(F\) is flasque, then for every locally closed subset \(Z\) of \(X\) and every integer \(i\neq 0\), we have \(\operatorname{H}^i_Z(X, F)=0\), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F)=0\). Conversely, if for every closed subset \(Z\) of \(X\) we have \(\operatorname{H}_Z^1(X, F)=0\), then \(F\) is flasque.

Suppose that \(F\) is flasque, then \(F\) induces a flasque sheaf on every open set, so that to prove \(\operatorname{H}^i_Z(X, F)=0\) for \(i>0\), we may suppose \(Z\) closed, and then the assertion follows from the exact sequence 2.9. We conclude, for every locally closed \(Z\), by "sheafifying", i.e. applying 2.4, that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F)=0\) for \(i>0\). Conversely, suppose \(\operatorname{H}^1_Z(X, F)=0\) for every closed \(Z\), then the exact sequence 2.9 proves that for every such \(Z\), \(\operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X-Z, F)\) is surjective, which means that \(F\) is flasque.

Combining 2.6 and 2.8, we shall deduce from them:

Proposition 2.13

Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(X\), \(Z\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(U=X-Z\), \(N\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F)=0\) for \(i\leqslant N\).

  2. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\), considering the canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(V\cap U, F), \]

    this homomorphism is:

    1. a)

      bijective for \(i<N\),

    2. b)

      injective for \(i=N\).

(When \(N>0\), one can in (ii) restrict to requiring a)).

To prove (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii), we are reduced, thanks to the local nature of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^i(F)\), to proving the

Corollary 2.14

If condition 2.13 (i) is satisfied, then

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(U, F) \]

is bijective for \(i<N\), injective for \(i=N\).

Indeed, by virtue of the exact sequence (27), this also means \(\operatorname{H}^i_Z(X, F)=0\) for \(i\leqslant N\), and this relation is an immediate consequence of the spectral sequence 2.6.

Conversely, hypothesis 2.13 (ii) means that for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), we have

\[ \operatorname{H}^i_{Z\cap V}(V, F|_{V}) =0 \text{ for } i\leqslant N, \]

which implies 2.13 (i) thanks to 2.4. If moreover \(N>0\), one can also say that (ii) a) implies (on passing to the associated sheaves) that \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(F|_{U})\) is an isomorphism, and that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_U^i(F)=0 \) for \(1\leqslant i<N\).

Taking into account 2.11, this proves once more 2.13 (i)...

Remark 30

Let \(Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a closed immersion, and suppose that locally it is of the form \(\{0\}\times Y \subset R^n \times Y\). Suppose that \(F\) is a locally constant sheaf on \(x\), then we find

\[ \tag{30} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i (F)\simeq \begin{cases} 0 &\text{if } i \neq n\\ F\otimes \underline{T}_{Y, X}& \text{if } i=n, \text{ where } \underline{T}_{Y, X} \simeq \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^n(\mathbf{Z}_X) \end{cases} \]

is a sheaf extension to \(X\) of a sheaf on \(Y\) locally isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}_Y\), called the "normal orientation sheaf of \(Y\) in \(X\)".

Using the spectral sequence 2.6, we find in this case:

\[ \tag{31} \operatorname{H}^i_Y(X, F)\simeq \operatorname{H}^{i-n}(Y, F\otimes \underline{T}_{Y, X}), \]

and we recover the "Gysin homomorphism":

\[ \tag{32} {\operatorname{H}^j(Y, F\otimes \underline{T}_{Y, X}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{j+n}(X, F)}. \]

Suppose that \(F\) is flasque, then \(F\) induces a flasque sheaf on every open set, so that to prove \(\operatorname{H}^i_Z(X, F)=0\) for \(i>0\), we may suppose \(Z\) closed, and then the assertion follows from the exact sequence 2.9. We conclude, for every locally closed \(Z\), by "sheafifying", i.e. applying 2.4, that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F)=0\) for \(i>0\). Conversely, suppose \(\operatorname{H}^1_Z(X, F)=0\) for every closed \(Z\), then the exact sequence 2.9 proves that for every such \(Z\), \(\operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X-Z, F)\) is surjective, which means that \(F\) is flasque.
Combining 2.6 and 2.8, we shall deduce from them:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.13

Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(X\), \(Z\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(U=X-Z\), \(N\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F)=0\) for \(i\leqslant N\).

  2. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\), considering the canonical homomorphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}^i(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(V\cap U, F), \]

    this homomorphism is:

    1. a)

      bijective for \(i<N\),

    2. b)

      injective for \(i=N\).

(When \(N>0\), one can in (ii) restrict to requiring a)).

To prove (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii), we are reduced, thanks to the local nature of the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^i(F)\), to proving the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.14

If condition 2.13 (i) is satisfied, then

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(U, F) \]

is bijective for \(i<N\), injective for \(i=N\).
Indeed, by virtue of the exact sequence (27), this also means \(\operatorname{H}^i_Z(X, F)=0\) for \(i\leqslant N\), and this relation is an immediate consequence of the spectral sequence 2.6.
Conversely, hypothesis 2.13 (ii) means that for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), we have
\[ \operatorname{H}^i_{Z\cap V}(V, F|_{V}) =0 \text{ for } i\leqslant N, \]

which implies 2.13 (i) thanks to 2.4. If moreover \(N>0\), one can also say that (ii) a) implies (on passing to the associated sheaves) that \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(F|_{U})\) is an isomorphism, and that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_U^i(F)=0 \) for \(1\leqslant i<N\).
Taking into account 2.11, this proves once more 2.13 (i)...

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 30

Let \(Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a closed immersion, and suppose that locally it is of the form \(\{0\}\times Y \subset R^n \times Y\). Suppose that \(F\) is a locally constant sheaf on \(x\), then we find

\[ \tag{30} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i (F)\simeq \begin{cases} 0 &\text{if } i \neq n\\ F\otimes \underline{T}_{Y, X}& \text{if } i=n, \text{ where } \underline{T}_{Y, X} \simeq \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^n(\mathbf{Z}_X) \end{cases} \]

is a sheaf extension to \(X\) of a sheaf on \(Y\) locally isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}_Y\), called the "normal orientation sheaf of \(Y\) in \(X\)".
Using the spectral sequence 2.6, we find in this case:
\[ \tag{31} \operatorname{H}^i_Y(X, F)\simeq \operatorname{H}^{i-n}(Y, F\otimes \underline{T}_{Y, X}), \]
and we recover the "Gysin homomorphism":
\[ \tag{32} {\operatorname{H}^j(Y, F\otimes \underline{T}_{Y, X}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{j+n}(X, F)}. \]
